turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Butler, you may proceed whenever you're ready.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This matter is before the Court on appeal of the decision by the Wisconsin Supreme Court upholding the constitutionality of a procedural rule requiring Appellate counsel to inform the Court why issues in an appeal lack merit when counsel reaches that conclusion. We contend in this case that the decision of ihe Wisconsin Supreme Court is in error and that the effeci of ihe Appellee's argument and the decision of the Wisconsin Supreme Court is to ask this Court to redefine either the role of counsel in an appeal or the nature of an appeal or both. Now, ---

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say that's the result of the Wisconsin Supreme Court's decision?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what's the matter with that result?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: In this particular case, the problem with that result is that, as Appellee concedes rn choir orref, it removes an appeal from the adversarial c e s l 1 ng p r oooss once counsel reaches the conclusion that there r s no me x* x l lo xhe appeal. In so doing, since Wisconsin has provided by its constitution that a client is entitled to an appeal a mat. i. er of right, he is getting something less than that in the Wisconsin judicial system.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, of course, indigent defendants who will get appeals as a matter of right as in Wisconsin are entitled to the benefit of counsel, but we have cases that sa'j they're not going to get everything that the well-heeled criminal defendant is going to get. It just isn't in the nature of things. Probably a well-heeled defendant can walk into some law office and plunk down enough money to get the lawyer to make any claim the defendant tells him to make. It's uafurtunate, but it's true. But certainly you don't contend that the indigent defendant is entit1ed to that sort of service, do you?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: We are not contending that the indigent defendant is en titied to everything. For example, I think you are correct that a client who has a great deal of money can shop around. I think that was recognized in the United States v. Edwards, 7th Circuit case. You can shop around and look for someone to make that argument. that, in fact, in discussing the due process rule, they've also would receive if he were having a retained counsel. So, the Court has made that recognition in the past. Basically, in this situation, Appellee concedes that the purpose of this rule is to take the appeal out of the adversarial testing process. Now, since the Court has previously described in Polk Countyv. Dodson and Evitts v. Lucey that an appeal is just that, it's the adversarial testing process, and that a client is deprived of due process when he has less than that. In effect, what they are asking the Court to do is, in a context of a no merit appeal, take the appeal out of the adversarial process, redefine the nature of appeal, and give Lite c lieni something less than that which he is entitled to as a matter of right in Wisconsin. Now, in addition --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I interrupt, Mr. Butler, right there? Assuming you just applied Anders as written and you don't have this additional requirement of a lawyer explaining why he thinks it's frivolous, do you think the normal Anders case in which the lawyer files an Anders brief, in which he makes the identifies the arguable arguments un behalf of his lhj.l na l Lakes i l oul ol Lhe adverserial process7

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: The one thing that Anders did — I do not. I think the one thing that Anders did —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You think that is the same advocacy that the rich person that the Chief Justice mentioned would get?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Right.'I think the one thing that Anders did is to try --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It is the same advocacy that the rich client would get? Do you think his client is going to go in and make some arguable things, saying yes, but I think it's frivolous and I'd like to withdraw?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: No. I'm sorry. I misunderstood your guestion. No. I can't imagine a client paying a lawyer to go in and argue the case as totally frivolous.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What we've got is a case where the paying client, if he's got a conscience lawyer, would say to him, you're going to waste your money. I'm telling you that, in advance. It's not worth $5,000 to file this. Of course, if you want to throw your money away, I'll file your papers for you.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What you're saying is that the pool’ defendant is entitled to have the state waste the same amount of money.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thai's correct.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: In effect, the poor defendant -- for example, In Anders, Anders tried to resolve a difficult ethical obliyated to litigate the appeal on the client's behalf to the best of one's ability, but, on the other hand, you have reached the decision that the case is wholly frivolous and without arguable merit. Now, the client is entitled to have the appeal as a matter of right and he's entitled to a vigorous advocate to put forth issues on his behalf. At the same time, the lawyer is confronted with --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But he's not under Anders. He's not under Anders entitled to that.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Under Anders, he is entitled to that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, he's not entitled to the vigorous advocacy. He's entitled to have the points identified, the colorable basis identified and then the lawyer is entitled to withdraw.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's should the Court reach the conclusion that the case is wholly frivolous. However, Anders does --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But he doesn't go in and make a vigorous argument don'! let me withdraw. He: files his brief with the arguable points in it and says I think these are frivolous.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then he's out and if the Court thinks there's anything in it, they appoint a new lawyer under Anders.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Right. The one problem I think with that analysis, I think Anders does require you to be an Ellis v. United States. Ellis_v. United States had a discussion requirement very similar to the one that the Wisconsin Supreme Court adopted in this case, and in that particular case, two different attorneys evaluated the appeal, cited possible issues to support the appeal, and then, after raising those issues, then proceeded to advise the Court why those issues lacked meidt . The United States Supreme Court reversed the decision of the United States Court of Appeals and held that that was not acting as an advocate on behalf of the client.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if he points out those arguable basis for the appeal, and then in the last line says I resign, please: let me out, and the Court lets him out, there's nothing wrong with that, is there?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's what Anders says. That's whal Anders says

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That is what Anders says. asking to resign or to get out?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: I don't think he should. I think at that point --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, it's just to be inferred that or —

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: No, no. I think —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's really inferred that he thinks it' frivolous.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Right. I think it ties into Justice Stevens' question. I think counsel is under Anders suppo i.O make a vigorous argument. I think as a argument, counsel must advise the Court that, in his opinion, he believes the argument he is about to make is frivolous, but he should still argue the case on behalf of the client.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that's the kiss of death in any real live court, is to say I'm going to make the following points and I intend to make them very vigorously, but I want to tell you beforehand that I think it's all a pile of junk. That's really what he's saying.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: By asking to resign.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: In Anders, that essentially it; correct. However, the value of that rule and the reason Anders is a difficult case for an appellate lawyer to defend, because you your opinion, the case is frivolous. However, by --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And we va sard rune rhur doeon l violal e the 6th Amendment.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct. for the lawyer to tell the Court why lie thinks it's frivolous?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: At that point, by telling the Court why the case is frivolous, the lawyer is doing more than just acknowledging that, in his opinion, the case is weak, but the: Court must make the determination. At that point, counsel is now providing the brief, in effect, for the other side. You are now briefing both sides of the case. Here's our case, here's the state's case. In my opinion, I'd side wi tli the state.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, a lawyer has an ethical obligation under some circumstances to bring to the Court's attention authorities on the other side, doesn't he?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or she?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: And that's why I believe that Anders resolved that ethical dilemma. Anders says in that situation, we give you permission to tell us that the case is frivolous, the current rules, have an obligation not to file frivolous papers

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct. And that's, in fact, the case in --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because they can be -- certainly, in l lie trial court, Rule 11 would catch them, isn't that right?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, I don’t know that you can say that even a well-paid -- even a paying client isn’t entitled to a lawyer who will file frivolous cases. The lawyer isn't supposed to do that.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: No. The lawyer is not supposed to do that, and we are not arguing that there are attorneys out thor who would be unethical.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It has been done. What about a case wher the lawyers argue the case and haven't read the briefs. The judge says, well, look, Io your only pari In this that the man was convicted on three counts and was sentenced on count two the only point. He said, why are you bringing it hero. Ho Well, all he wasted was five minutes of the Court's

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct. In fact, the irony of the Wisconsin Supreme Court's decision, if it had really -- saves the Court no time whatsoever. The only agency that is saved any time by the Anders procedure is the agency representing the State of Wisconsin. The Court in either case has to evaluate the arguments that are presented to it by Appellate counsel, whether it's a meritorious appeal or whether it's a no merit appeal. The public defender or the appointed Appel1a1e counsel who is representing the defendant has to brief the issues in either situation, advise the client of the possible ramifications to the appeal, his analysis of the merits of the appeal, and whether or not there's a possibility of success, and then present those arguments to the Court. The only one who doesn't have to do anything in this because, now, appointed Appellate's counsel is doing their job for them. We are now briefing the case for the state. positiun that they do not have to respond and so far that is the rule of law in Wisconsin. So, you are not anticipating getting a response from the Slate of Wisconsin in an Anders situation. The only one actually benefit the client in some cases because I've had cases where I thought the answer was perfectly clear and then I started to write out the reasons why it's clear and I find, well, it isn't really all that clear, and if you come to a conclusion that something is frivolous, say, oh, there's nothing to this case, and then you have to explain why, you're going to be darn sure it is frivolous. It's easy sometimes in a big record to say that I don't think there's anything here. So, doesn't the requ.i reinenl of articulating the basis for the conclusion it's frivolous actually provide some protection to the client?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Actually, it does not because by providing the Court with those reasons, assuming that you've situation is told the Court why the case lacks merit. It's our position that if it is not readily apparent from the face of the Anders brief why that case Is frivolous, then perhaps counsel was wrong In making that determination. to spoil out satisfactory reasons and lte'11 change his mind. But if he can just file it without stating the reasons, he's

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: But the answer to that, Justice Stevens, is that in that situation, the Court can order the opposing side to brief the case for the other side. You don't need for process. You still have an advocate's brief on behalf of 1 he client. The issues are still highlighted.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It seems to me in that case, it would be better for the lawyer to find out himself that he was initially wrong in his judgment of frivolity or frivolousness, whatever the right word is, and to correct it himself and say', no, there is some merit to it, rather than filing an Anders brief wiihout an explanation and then having the state say, well, ho acknowledges frivolous, so that's it.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Stevens, I think a lawyer does that when he makes the analysis of the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you hope he does.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: The problem is not with making the - analysis for the case. The problem is within providing that analysis —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if he does it, how often does he do briefs in my life. I question whether I should have filed Giiher one or ■ rn.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In those cases, do you think it would ha\ really made any difference if you'd included the statement of reasons?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: In one of the cases, in fuel, I did include a statement of the reasons at my client's direction, and I do think it made a difference because his case came subsequent to this one. His conviction has already been affirmed. That is one of the unique things about the Wisconsii rule*. You don't dismiss an appeal in Wisconsin if the Court accepts an Anders brief. You affirm the conviction. So, he has an appeal, but it's not the same type of appeal that's contemplated by the Constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, except the Constitution doesn't really contemplate an awful lot of frivolous appeals. I mean, — well, anyway.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: No. I agree the Constitution does not contemplate a lot of frivolous appeals. I think counsel's responsibility is to try to argue on behalf of his client cl S best as possible. In the unlikely event that you do have or the uniqu event thai you do have a frivolous appeal, you're still obligated until you are allowed to withdraw from the ease to protect that appeal. You still represent the client, and to the extent if there is a conflict between a client's ethical responsibility and a client's legal responsibility to the client under the Constitution, it's our1 contention that the We are not supposed to be an administrative aid to the Court. Polk County v._Dodson, the Court, made it clear. The client is supposed to have the undivided loyalty of the attorney, and what the Wisconsin Supreme Court has admittedly done in admitting that it gees beyond Anders is it says, no, you have two duties in this case. Only one is to the client, the other is to the Court, and we want you to wear1 both hois . the same time, and that provides the client, and this goes to the second part of the argument, with the redefining of the role of counsel, that provides the client with less than an advocate. You are not being an advocate. You are being a counselor as Appellee as pointed out --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but you're arguing —. you say he's entitled to the undivided loyalty of the lawyer. Wei 1, the lawyer gives him his undivided loyalty and when he says to no] I recognize you want to appeal but there is absolutely (o r to this appeal, it's a waste of time and money and ever ay

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: There's nothing inconsistent with Lhai.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then he goes on and he says, moreover, since it's frivolous, I have an ethical duty not to prosecute it and to advise the Court of it. Why is that inconsistent with his duty to his client?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Because the client at that point.says, I have a right to an appeal and I want the appeal, and at that point, the lawyer's duty is to protect that appeal. His constitutional duty. Because he has the right to appeal as a ma 11er of right, no t as a ma11er of privilege.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Wisconsin can wel1 look at it a different way, that the reason he's setting forth the causes for frivolousness are not to harm his client, but, rather, to justify to the Court his withdrawal from the case. Now, there do arise situations where a lawyer has two obligations; one to the Court, one to the client. In justifying his actions to the Court, it seems to me he is certainly being no less loyal to the client that by the mere fact of his saying I will not take an appeal. I mean, at that point, he's air e a d y betrayed client if you consider that a betrayal. From there on, al 1 he's doing is justifying to the Court the reas on for hi If you liken this to a trial situation, this would be akin to a lawyer coming in on the eve of trial, evaluating !he case, telling the client it's obvious that you're guilty, obvious that we don't have a prayer, we ought to plead and take the best deal possible, and the client says, no, I want a trial, and the lawyer says, no, I'm going to plead you guilty.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Butler, I think there is a good deal of difference between a trial situation where the burden. of proof is always on the Government and any competent a! l.omey knows that he represents the defendant by simply putting the: Government to its proof, just by cross examining, and on the other hand, on appeal, you get Into situations, which I daresay you've confronted some yourself having filed two Anders, where you have to -- you know, the burden of proof is no longer on the Government, the burden of proof is on the Appellant, and there just isn't much — there's virtually nothing to comp.la in trial court did. The jury still returned a verdict of yuiliy.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Rehnquist, I think your question points to the very reason why this case is here. Tt is precisely because in-an appellate situation that the burden is now on the defendant. Precisely because a defendant now needs a lawyer to act as a sword to overturn that appeal, it is for that very reason that the role of counsel becomes critical in appeal. It's critical at that point. He needs help, tor;: netti' there so that he can come in and try to overturn the decision; otherwise, the judgment of guilt is already in, it;: has already been sentenced, and there is nothing further ihai can be: done on his behalf. That is the reason why counsel is cri!ical on appeal

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but you would agree, I take it, that i here are some appeals, I don't know how .large a class i! it-;, that even the best lawyer in the world has virtually no chance of getting — of succeeding on; whereas, it's not nearly as easy to evaluate that in the trial situation. Everybody agree that abandoning a client or saying I think your defense is frivolous, therefore I won't represent you in trial, it just can't be done. It isn't done. It isn't a problem. Rehnquist. That's why we are defending the Anders decision, because Anders came down with a compromise, that allowed the already assessed lacks merit. You have to make* meritorious arguments to the best of your ability in that situation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, don’t you recognise that every lawyer has two loyalties, in effect; one to the client and eme­ to the Court? Isn't that right?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Yes, but it's the duty —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, that's right, and it seems to sue that, the Court can ask the lawyer to tell the Court why ihe lawyer is taking a certain position. In this case, the Court has made a universal question, tell me why.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: I think in that situation, it can only do so if it redefines the role of either the appeal ox- counsel in that settiny because as long as this Court has taken the position that a client is entitled to the adversarial process in an appeal, and as long as this Court has taken the position that the client is entitled to an advocate, to then say, but in this narrow realm of cases, we're going to give these clients less than that is giving that class of clients 1 ess than the are receiving. So, in effect, the Court is asking us to wear two hats, to brief both sides, to be more of an administrat ivo a 1d to the Court, ami actually to argue as an amicus curiae, and this Court in Anders, in Ellis, in Evills, and all other decisions discussing this issue, has said counsel cannot do that, counsel cannot act as an amicus curiae. The i rony of the state's position and the Wiscuusi Supreme Court dec is ion in this case: is that it's far less onerous to argue or to take the position of the dissent in Anders as it is to take the position of the Wisconsin Supreme: Court in this case because, at least in that setting, the client still had the appeal because the client can still come forward and make, absence of counsel, he can make his own frivolous arguments and still have a full appeal on the merits In this setting, the client does not receive that. Ke receives less than an appeal. He receives less than advocacy, and it is our contention that to adopt this prueedur Is to provide this class of clients with less than that ihey'r entitled to under Wisconsin law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think Justice Abrahamson, who dissented below, was very happy with the Anders situation?

turns.name: BUTI.ER
turns.text: I'm not sure she was. T think that was one of the reasons that she called for in her dissent a. re- evaluation of the rule. There are obviously problems to this dilemma that Appellate counsel faced, but I am not. aware of an position that protects the rights of the client and the ethica duties of the lawyer better than Anders. So, i i: is our position that we would ask that Court reaffirm Anders and reverse the Wisconsin Supreme Court. I would ask to reservo the remainder of my time for rebuttal.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Butler. Mr. Kleinmaier, we'll hear from you now.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, arid may it please the Court: It is ironic that in an attack on the Wisconsin procedure, which requires the defense attorney to explain the reasons for reaching a conclusion an appeal is frivolous, the case is based on the — the argument is based on Anders because in the Anders case, the petitioner himself, one of the remedies he thought would be better than the situation he faced was to have the defense attorney provide an explanation. As quoted, the source of this is a footnote in Wickols, the Nickols v._Gagnon case. The brief on behalf of the Petitioner in Anders recommended a procedure in the District of Columbia Court of Appeals because it afforded more adequate protection because counsel must convince the Court that the issues are trxily frivolous. That's what essentially the discussion requiromo the Wisconsin rule is. The defense attorney who has stated — set forth the facts and the law then asks — states 1! !s frivolous and seeks to withdraw and all this discussion requirement does it ask him to explain why he reached that conclusion, and in making that requirement on the attorney, T think that is completely consistent with the Anders decision a nd --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the state's procedure here satisfy the record that arguably supports his client?

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Yes, sir. As part of the procedur in Wisconsin also, the attorney cites the facts and the legal authority.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then he says, but this is frivolous?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because these things are — tiit.se arguments are so baseless, they're frivolous, is that it?

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Yes. The Wisconsin procedure was the Court's attempt. The rule is a codification of the Wisconsin case of Cleghorn_v. State, which was a Wisconsin Court's attempt to implement the Anders decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Justice Harlan in Anders has said that you can't really point — if you can point to anything that's arguable, it just isn't frivolous.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: That's a difficult part of implementing the Anders decision, and I think what maybe whai required is that the defense attorney review the case and he finds Li 's frivolous, and then he or she Lias to look a 1 Li and those tilings — when you review a record that you find is frivolous, there arc certainly going to be tilings that draw your aitent ion, you check out , because it just doesn't seem — seems out of tlie ordinary. In reviewing the record and complying with Anders, the attorney would then point out, I think, the things trial — the best arguments that could be found, and —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Anders didn't say on its face that you weren't entitled not only to satisfy what the Cour Irl attorney had to say, but it didn't say that -- it didn't hold that the — that it was impermissible for tlie lawyer to explain wLiy lie thought it was frivolous.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Right. It did not make a point of it either way. In fact, in a footnote, it cited the District of Columbia rule and said there Weis not approving or disapproving of that rule in the Anders case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I suppose the case that 's arguably more against this Wisconsin procedure is Ellis.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Well, as I recall —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's a short precarium, but 11: doesn't say very much.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: And that's the problem, I think, that it doesn't say very much because in reading the case, we I think a distinct ion between an amicus and an advocate, the advocate obviously is attempting to serve his there's merit or whether it's frivolous, he has to review all client and, if, in reviewing it that way, he can find something that he can make a legitimate argument on, then he should pursue the appeal. -i agreed the appeal was not frivolous? That's the difference between that case and these others.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: But I think that — the attorneys who had reviewed it had originally reached a conclusion, T think, based on the argument that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Solici tor- General agreed when the case was before us that It was not a frivolous case.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: But I think a case would be one where I think someone could look at it .arid say, you're probably going to lose, but li's not frivolous because you have a good argument, but you may still lose it. I think in serving your client as an advocate, you're going to look at a case: and say, you may have a chance — you may lose, but there's a very good argument that we should pursue. that * s part of the whole process, because if the state; is brief filed for the client, the defendant; a brief filed for full-fledged appeal, whereas this la part of a process i na i recognizer that frivolous cases should not be brought — followed up on a full appeal, and this is a method of re! loving the attorney from representation before lhe appeal is brought.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why should they be relieved?

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: So that the — the defense lawyer? Because the appeal is frivolous and this Court has also frivolous appeals. file briefs in those cases?

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Not in Wisconsin. If the Court — the defense lawyer files the no merit report, if the Court finds merit, then it's sent back to the attorney or a new attorney and the case is briefed and then it follows' through regular appeals. The state would file a brief, of course. Bui, no, the state does not file any response to the no mer ti

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, there's no appeal?

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why should the Court have to determine whether there is or is nut merit after an Anders brief is filed? Why isn't that your job?

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Well, this Court said in Anders that the Court has the responsibility to make the independent determination whether the appeal ris attorney.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Yes, there is some benefit, but, again, I think the purpose of this is to avoid an appeal because the issue is determined as frivolous, and if you have the state responding, that is an appeal. I mean, that’s the way the — the full appeal in Wisconsin, there — in the Court of Appeals, I think in criminal cases, I think In .ill casos, the Court of Appeals, there are very few oral arguments and the entire appeal constitutes briefs being filed by each side and the Coui'i reaching a decision. So, another question, I guess, is if the state — if the Attorney General's office is involved on behalf of the state, what: kind of brief was filed. Is this one — what you have is a report from the defense attorney saying the case is frivolous. Is the state then supposed to respond and say no, it's not frivolous? The state's posit ion would normally be that that 's correc i .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask, before this rule — how long has the rule been in its present form?

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: I think since 1978.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you happen to know whether before 1978, when an Antler a brief was filed without an explanat ion of why i was frivolous, did the state then respond?

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: No, it did not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, that hasn't changed. T mean, in eiihe event, once the counsel for the Appellant has represented it» the Court there's a frivolous appeal, the: Attorney General's office says this is not a case to spend a lot of time on. Don't file anything.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: I ' rn not sure if even before this rule, if the briefs — if the no merit reports were even filed with the Attorney General's office. I think they were filed with the Court. I'm not sure about that point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you know where we could find or I coul find an example of a brief that satisfies the Wisconsin rule? Because the one that was filed in Mr1. McCoy' s case here, I guess, he said he purported to comply with Anders but left out the explanation of frivolity.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Right. I —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's the way this original proceeding got started.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Right. To get a brief filed in compliance with that rule, the only source that I would think of would be the Clerk's office for the Wisconsin courts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say that*it's possible to file a letter that satisfies both Anders and the Wisconsin rule, I lake ii? Don'1 you? T thought i fia l wh at you said to withdraw, and here's why I think it's frivolous.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: That's right. We acknowledge tha it is in addition to Anders. I mean, Anders itself does not

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you think it 's possible to write a letter1 that satisfies both Anders and the Wisconsin rule?

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Well, I think that if you satisfy the Wisconsin rule, you will — you have satisfied Anders because the Wisconsin rule requires the same thing as Anders and, in addition, specifically requires the attorney explain

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But there is nothing in this case and the It: L ter —

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: No, it's not a situation where ih evidence was introduced that would have precluded.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, we really are sori of dealing in an abstraction.

turns.name: STEPHEN W. KLEINMAIER
turns.text: Yes. In that regard, yes. The Wisconsin Supreme Court also pointed out one o the rationales for this, arid I think if is a f a e i , and l his also consistent with Anders, that the documents submitted by Lite au merit repor L io of assistance Lo Lite Couri: and assistance to the Court was one of the policies or one t; f the Lhinys that Lite Court in Anders said was to be accwmp 1 ish;;d by the report, and this requirement Lo assist the Court by he1piny i I: in its independent review it has to undertake, - but it will be of assistance to get the analysis from the — the evai ui i 1 L O l 1 from the defense attorney also. I think that concludes the argument.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Kloinmaier. Mr. Bui lei', you have: four minutes remaining.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. Very briefly, one of the arguments advanced by ih Assistant Attorney Getier.il in this case Is to argue that !b is no way any so-called no merit letter to determine if an attorney acts as an advocate on behalf of the cl lent . I would point out to this Court that this case do not present that situation. This is a case where counsel ha identified issues on behalf of the client, so the Court is better position to determine whether or not counsel's assessment is accurate based on the identification of those issue This is not the same as the situation that presented in Anders. la addition, the alate hay conceded once ayain here in the argument that a client will nut receive a full appeal in Wisconsin consti Lutional pruviyicm that provideo t hat all criminal defendants receive* an appeal as a mat ter of right .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, certainly the Supreme Court erf Wisconsin didn't think it was contrary to the Wisconsin Constitution or they wouldn't have said it was all right .

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct, but the Wisconsin. Supreme Court in making that ruling basically admitted that it was going beyond the requirements of Anders and that ii was asking an at torney to do something more: than remain as an advocate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that is undoubtedly a federal constitutional question. But I don'i see how you can rely here on a violation of the Wisconsin Constitution when 1 hr: Supreme Court of Wisconsin has upheld the thing.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: No, I'm not arguing that it's si raci ly a violation of the Wisconsin Constitution, from that standpoint . Our argument is that by requiring an attorney to act as less than an advocate, which .is what the 6th Amendment requires, and by requiring an attorney, once the state provides for an appeal as a matter of right, to provide the client with less than 1 hai appeal, then the 14th Amendment and the 6th Amendment of ihe United States Constitution have been violated. ■ tiling . You s ay that in this case, the lawyer d J. cl i J. IlCl * lit.'JT'vri were arguable claims that could have been assiar i ocl oh appcl . Are those i n the record, what those points were? MR . BUTLER: The issues that have binon identified ar in the record. The issues that were identified, the ai tori t:Y < frivolous and lack arguable merit. However, here they are.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But can you tell me where in the record those issues are identified?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: Those are in the joint appendix.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you the one wiio filed the Anders letter?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And said that you couldn' 1 real 1y — and you left out the explanation of frivolity?

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: That's correct. It's in the joint appendix, page 11.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thank yuu very much.

turns.name: LOUIS BENNETT BUTLER, JR.
turns.text: If there are no further quest ions, thank you very much.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Butler. The case is submitted.. We'll argument next in Case Number 86—1461, Edward J.
